title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
full_text:
1. The question whether the right to privacy is a fundamental right under the Constitution has been answered in the affirmative.

2. Privacy includes at its core the preservation of personal intimacies, the sanctity of family life, marriage, procreation, the home and sexual orientation. Privacy also connotes a right to be left alone.

3. The right to privacy is protected as an intrinsic part of the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21 and as a part of the freedoms guaranteed by Part III of the Constitution.

4. Privacy is not an absolute right. Like other rights which form part of the fundamental freedoms protected by Part III, including the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21, privacy is not an absolute right. A law which encroaches upon privacy will have to withstand the touchstone of permissible restrictions on fundamental rights.

5. An invasion of life or personal liberty must meet the threefold requirement of (i) legality, which postulates the existence of law; (ii) need, defined in terms of a legitimate state aim; and (iii) proportionality which ensures a rational nexus between the objects and the means adopted to achieve them.

6. Informational privacy is a facet of the right to privacy. The dangers to privacy in an age of information can originate not only from the state but also from non-state actors.

7. The right to privacy is an inherent part of the fundamental right to life and liberty guaranteed by Article 21 of the Constitution. This right is available to all individuals and is not merely a privilege of the wealthy.

title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
full_text:
1. The right to travel abroad is a part of the right to personal liberty under Article 21 of the Constitution.

2. No person can be deprived of his right to travel abroad except according to procedure established by law. The procedure prescribed by law must satisfy the test of reasonableness.

3. Article 21 does not exclude Articles 14 and 19. The procedure established by law must be right, just and fair and not arbitrary, fanciful or oppressive.

4. The expression 'procedure established by law' in Article 21 means fair, just and reasonable procedure. The procedure which deprives a person of his life or personal liberty must be reasonable, fair and just.

5. There is an intimate connection between Articles 14, 19 and 21 of the Constitution. Any law depriving a person of personal liberty has to stand the test of all three articles.

6. The right under Article 21 is the most fundamental of all rights. It cannot be taken away except by procedure established by law, and that procedure must be just, fair and reasonable.

7. Natural justice is a component of fair procedure under Article 21. The audi alteram partem rule applies to all proceedings including administrative proceedings.

title: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
full_text:
1. The Parliament has the power to amend any provision of the Constitution including the fundamental rights, but the Parliament cannot alter the basic structure of the Constitution.

2. The basic structure doctrine means that certain features of the Constitution are so essential that they cannot be abrogated or destroyed by amendment. These include supremacy of the Constitution, republican and democratic form of government, secular character, separation of powers, and federal character.

3. Article 368 does not enable Parliament to alter the basic structure or framework of the Constitution. The power to amend is not a power to destroy.

4. The fundamental rights guaranteed under Part III of the Constitution can be amended, but the amendment must not damage or destroy the basic structure.

5. Judicial review is a basic feature of the Constitution. The courts have the power to examine the constitutionality of legislative and executive actions.

6. The doctrine of basic structure is a safeguard against the abuse of the amending power. It ensures that the identity of the Constitution is preserved.

title: State of Rajasthan v. Balchand
full_text:
1. The basic rule is bail, not jail, except where there are circumstances suggestive of fleeing from justice or thwarting the course of justice or creating other troubles in the form of repeating offences or intimidating witnesses.

2. Bail is the rule and jail is the exception. The court should lean in favour of granting bail particularly when the accused has no prior criminal record.

3. Personal liberty under Article 21 of the Constitution is a precious right. Deprivation of personal liberty, even for a single day, is one too many.

4. The considerations for granting bail include: (a) nature and seriousness of the offence; (b) character of the evidence; (c) circumstances peculiar to the accused; (d) possibility of the accused fleeing from justice; (e) possibility of repetition of offence.

5. Refusal of bail is a restriction on the personal liberty of the individual guaranteed under Article 21 of the Constitution. The court should not refuse bail mechanically.

6. The detention of an undertrial prisoner for an unreasonable period amounts to violation of Article 21 of the Constitution.

title: National Legal Services Authority v. Union of India
full_text:
1. Transgender persons have the right to decide their self-identified gender. The recognition of transgenders as a third gender is not a social or medical issue but a human rights issue.

2. The right to equality under Article 14, prohibition of discrimination under Article 15, and protection of life and personal liberty under Article 21 include within their ambit the rights of transgender persons.

3. Non-recognition of the identity of hijras/transgender persons denies them equal protection of law, thereby violating Articles 14 and 21 of the Constitution.

4. Every person has the right to gender identity. The court directed the government to treat transgender persons as socially and educationally backward classes and extend all kinds of reservation.

5. Discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation or gender identity impairs equality before law and equal protection of law guaranteed under Article 14 of the Constitution.

6. The court directed the Centre and State Governments to take steps to treat them as socially and educationally backward classes of citizens and extend all kinds of reservation in cases of admission in educational institutions and for public appointments.

title: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
full_text:
1. Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, insofar as it criminalises consensual sexual conduct between adults of the same sex, is violative of Articles 14, 15, 19 and 21 of the Constitution.

2. The right to sexuality, the right to sexual autonomy and the right to choice of a sexual partner are part of the right to life under Article 21. Sexual orientation is an innate, immutable characteristic of every individual.

3. Consensual sexual acts of adults are entitled to the protection of the right to privacy which is a fundamental right under Article 21.

4. Section 377 is manifestly arbitrary because it criminalises a natural and immutable trait of an individual. What is natural to one, cannot be unnatural in any constitutional sense.

5. The majority of the court overruled the decision in Suresh Kumar Koushal v. NAZ Foundation (2014) and held that sexual orientation is a fundamental right protected under Articles 14, 15, 19 and 21.

6. History owes an apology to the members of the community for the delay in providing redressal for the ignominy and ostracism they have suffered through the centuries.

title: Smt. Shanti v. State of Haryana
full_text:
1. Section 304B of the Indian Penal Code deals with dowry death. Where the death of a woman is caused by any burns or bodily injury or occurs otherwise than under normal circumstances within seven years of her marriage and it is shown that soon before her death she was subjected to cruelty or harassment by her husband or any relative of her husband for, or in connection with, any demand for dowry, such death shall be called dowry death.

2. The essential ingredients of Section 304B IPC are: (a) the death of a woman must have been caused by burns or bodily injury or otherwise than under normal circumstances; (b) such death must have occurred within seven years of her marriage; (c) she must have been subjected to cruelty or harassment by her husband or any relative of her husband; (d) such cruelty or harassment must be for or in connection with demand for dowry.

3. Once the prosecution establishes these essentials, the court shall presume that such person has caused the dowry death. The burden of proof shifts to the accused under Section 113B of the Indian Evidence Act.

4. The expression 'soon before her death' used in Section 304B IPC is a relative term. It conveys the idea that the interval should not be much between the cruelty or harassment concerned and the death in question.

5. Continuous harassment or cruelty before death is required. Isolated acts of cruelty may not be sufficient unless there is a pattern of dowry-related harassment.

title: Aishat Shifa v. State of Karnataka
full_text:
1. The question before the court was whether the prescription of school uniform by the State Government which did not include hijab as part of the uniform violated the fundamental rights of Muslim girl students.

2. Justice Hemant Gupta held that wearing of hijab is not an essential religious practice in Islam and government has the power to prescribe uniform. The Government Order prescribing uniform was not violative of Article 25.

3. Justice Sudhanshu Dhulia dissented, holding that asking a girl student to remove her hijab before entering the school gate is an invasion of her privacy and dignity. The focus should be on education and not on what a student wears.

4. The matter was referred to a larger bench due to the split verdict. The question of essential religious practice test and its applicability to Article 25 claims remains open.

5. The case raised important questions about the intersection of religious freedom, right to education, and the power of the State to regulate dress codes in educational institutions.

6. Freedom of religion under Article 25 is subject to public order, morality and health, and to the other provisions of Part III of the Constitution.

title: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
full_text:
1. Sexual harassment at the workplace is a violation of the fundamental rights of gender equality and the right to life and liberty under Articles 14, 15 and 21 of the Constitution.

2. The court laid down detailed guidelines to be treated as law declared by the Supreme Court under Article 141 of the Constitution for the prevention and redressal of sexual harassment at the workplace.

3. The Vishaka Guidelines define sexual harassment to include unwelcome sexually determined behaviour such as physical contact and advances, demand or request for sexual favours, sexually coloured remarks, showing pornography, or any other unwelcome physical, verbal or non-verbal conduct of a sexual nature.

4. It is the duty of the employer to prevent or deter the commission of acts of sexual harassment and to provide the procedures for the resolution, settlement or prosecution of acts of sexual harassment.

5. Every employer must establish a Complaints Committee at the workplace to deal with complaints of sexual harassment. The Committee should be headed by a woman and at least half its members should be women.

6. The Guidelines were given the force of law until suitable legislation was enacted. Subsequently, the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 was enacted based on these guidelines.

title: Supriyo v. Union of India
full_text:
1. The court held that the Special Marriage Act, 1954 cannot be read down or interpreted to include same-sex marriages within its scope. The legislative intent was clearly to provide for marriage between a man and a woman.

2. However, the court unanimously recognized that queer persons are entitled to the full range of constitutional rights including the right to cohabit, the right to choose a partner, the right to form intimate associations without discrimination.

3. The right to enter into a union is a fundamental right. However, the court held that the right to marriage as a fundamental right was not yet established and the matter should be left to the legislature.

4. The Chief Justice and Justice Kaul, in their opinion, held that the right to form a civil union was a right flowing from Articles 19 and 21 and directed the government to constitute a committee to examine the rights of queer couples.

5. The court recognized that transgender persons in heterosexual relationships have the right to marry under existing laws.

6. While declining to judicially recognize same-sex marriage, the court emphasized that LGBTQ+ persons cannot be discriminated against or subjected to any disability on the ground of their sexual orientation.

title: Arnab Goswami v. State of Maharashtra
full_text:
1. The Supreme Court granted interim bail to the petitioner, observing that the High Court had erred in refusing to exercise its jurisdiction under Section 439 of the Code of Criminal Procedure.

2. The court reiterated that personal liberty under Article 21 is a sacrosanct right. Courts must exercise their jurisdiction of granting bail with due regard to the constitutional guarantee of personal liberty.

3. If courts do not intercede to protect personal liberty, the right to liberty would become illusory. The court observed that denial of bail amounts to deprivation of personal liberty.

4. The court held that the prima facie case must be assessed on the basis of the material on record. The FIR and the investigation material did not establish the ingredients of the offence of abetment of suicide.

5. Human liberty is a precious constitutional value. It cannot be sacrificed at the altar of conjecture and surmise. Courts must be vigilant in protecting the right to personal liberty.

6. The court emphasized that High Courts should exercise their power under Section 439 CrPC liberally and not create additional conditions that are not warranted by law.

title: Centre for Public Interest Litigation v. Union of India
full_text:
1. The Supreme Court cancelled 122 telecom licences allocated by the then Minister of Communications A. Raja on a first-come-first-served basis, holding that the process was arbitrary, capricious, and contrary to public interest.

2. Natural resources belong to the people. The State and its instrumentalities are trustees of natural resources. They cannot give them away to private parties at their sweet will.

3. The distribution of natural resources by the State must be guided by the principles of equality, fairness, and non-arbitrariness under Article 14 of the Constitution.

4. The court held that the first-come-first-served policy adopted for grant of UAS licences in 2007-08 was wholly arbitrary and contrary to public interest.

5. The court directed that spectrum and other natural resources of similar nature must be allocated through a fair and transparent process, including auction, which is the most efficient method.

6. The judgment established the principle that allocation of scarce national resources must follow a constitutionally valid process of auction or competitive bidding unless there are compelling reasons to deviate.
